title: Pregnancy and herbal medicine
subtitle: How to think about safety in the first, second, and third trimester
excerpt: A foundations article on using herbal preparations during pregnancy, which herbs are widely accepted, which require caution, which to avoid, and how to think about 'natural means safe'. Includes a practical guide to the pregnancy safety categories used across this section.
sourceNotes: EMA HMPC monographs for individual herbs, pregnancy safety classifications drawn from individual monographs. WHO Traditional Medicine Strategy 2014, 2023, safety of traditional medicine in special populations. Romm A., Botanical Medicine for Women's Health (2010), clinical herbalism pregnancy safety framework. Mills S. and Bone K., The Essential Guide to Herbal Safety (2005), detailed herb-specific safety classifications.

attrs.body: This article is an educational overview of how to think about herbal safety in pregnancy. It is not a substitute for advice from your midwife, GP, or a qualified herbalist who knows your individual circumstances. When in doubt, ask.
attrs.title: Not medical advice

content.text: Pregnancy is where the idea that 'natural means safe' does the most harm. Many herbs that suit a non-pregnant adult carry real concerns in pregnancy. The issue is not that they are toxic. It is that they can act on the womb, on hormone systems, or on stages of the baby's growth. This reading explains how to read the safety notes in this section. It sets out the three main categories of concern and lists the herbs you are most likely to meet at home, with guidance on use.

are herbs that bring on a period. In the old domestic herbal, 'bringing on the terms' was a coded way of saying ending an early pregnancy. Several herbs in this section (fennel, mugwort, pennyroyal, blue cohosh) carry that classification in the old books. The concern is at full medicinal dose (several cups a day of a strong preparation), not at cooking strength (a fennel seed in a salad, a sprig of thyme in a sauce). This section flags pregnancy caution where the old classification applies. Cooking strength is fine. A daily strong preparation is one to talk over with a midwife.

are herbs that bring on contractions of the womb. They sit in the higher-concern column. Blue cohosh, pennyroyal, tansy, and savin (juniper berry at high dose) are the most named in the old books and are not in this section. Raspberry leaf is the one you will meet most often at home. It is widely used from 32 weeks to tone the womb before labour. It is treated as safe from that point but is not for the first or second trimester. This section leaves raspberry leaf out because the timing point needs more personal guidance than a recipe card can give.

Most herbs have never been formally tested for safety in human pregnancy. Trials in pregnant people are rare for obvious reasons. The lack of evidence of harm is not the same as evidence that there is no harm. For herbs in this group, the guidance in this section follows tradition and a cautious line. Herbs with long records of use in pregnancy without reports of harm (nettle, ginger at low dose, chamomile at cooking strength) are marked as accepted with caution. Herbs without such a track record are marked 'discuss with midwife'.
Herbs generally accepted in pregnancy at low doses

These herbs appear in this section and are widely treated as fine in pregnancy at cooking to tea strength. Ginger up to 1 g of dried equivalent a day for morning sickness, with European medicines and WHO records to support it. Chamomile, one or two cups of standard tea a day, not daily strong medicinal preparations. Nettle leaf as a daily nutritive tonic, a long-standing pregnancy tradition. Elderflower infusion at standard tea strength. Lavender in the bath or as an infusion at normal strength. In every case the line is the same: tea strength is fine, daily strong medicinal preparations are not.
Herbs to discuss with a midwife during pregnancy

Fennel, with weak oestrogen-like activity and a period-bringing flag at medicinal dose. St John's Wort, not studied in pregnancy and with several drug interactions. Valerian tincture, for its sedative strength and alcohol content. Echinacea, an immune stimulant with limited pregnancy data. None of these is a flat no in every case. They are herbs where personal context matters enough that the answer depends on the person, not just the herb.
The first trimester is the most cautious period

Organ formation happens in the first 12 weeks. This is when traditional herbalists treat even safer herbs with extra care. The second and third trimesters are more open to herbs with good track records, because the baby is no longer in the organ-forming stage. That does not mean anything goes after the first 12 weeks. It does explain why some notes in this section say 'first trimester caution' rather than a blanket no.

Your midwife is the first point of call for any question about a specific herb in pregnancy. A qualified medical herbalist (MNIMH or MCPP registered in the UK) can give more detailed personal advice. The Herbal Safety website, run by the National Institute of Medical Herbalists, holds current safety summaries for common herbs. For anything beyond an occasional cup of a well-known herb, the answer is the same: ask someone who knows both herbalism and your own pregnancy.
